diagrams.title: Smart Contract Lifecycle
diagrams.description: Complete lifecycle of a smart contract from creation to execution and termination
elements: Contract Authoring (writing code in Solidity/Vyper) → Compilation (bytecode generation) → Deployment (transaction to blockchain) → Execution (triggered by conditions/events) → Termination (self-destruct or completion)
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete journey of a smart contract from development to execution on blockchain

diagrams.title: Smart Contract Architecture
diagrams.description: Internal structure showing components of a deployed smart contract
elements: Blockchain Layer (Ethereum VM), Contract Code (bytecode), Contract Storage (state variables), ABI Interface (Application Binary Interface), External Accounts (triggering transactions), Gas Metering (execution cost calculation)
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how smart contracts are stored, executed, and interact with the blockchain infrastructure

diagrams.title: Smart Contract Execution Flow
diagrams.description: Step-by-step flow showing how a smart contract executes when triggered
elements: User/External Account initiates transaction → Transaction signed with private key → Transaction broadcast to network → Validators include in block → EVM executes contract code → State changes recorded → Gas deducted from sender → Execution result returned
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete execution pathway and gas mechanism in smart contract execution

diagrams.title: Smart Contract vs Traditional Contract
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison of smart contracts and traditional legal contracts
elements: Traditional: Manual enforcement, intermediaries (lawyers/notaries), paper-based, slow, subjective interpretation | Smart Contract: Auto-execution, no intermediaries, digital code, instant, deterministic outcomes
diagrams.purpose: Students understand advantages of smart contracts over traditional contractual approaches

diagrams.title: Smart Contract Types Classification
diagrams.description: Classification of smart contracts based on functionality and network type
elements: By Platform: Ethereum, Hyperledger, Polkadot | By Function: Financial (DeFi), NFTs, Supply Chain, Voting, Insurance | By Network: Public, Private, Consortium | By Complexity: Simple (single function), Complex (multi-function DAOs)
diagrams.purpose: Students learn different categories and real-world applications of smart contracts

charts.title: Smart Contract Trigger and Execution Flowchart
charts.content: START → User sends transaction with function call → Check if contract exists → Verify signature → Load contract code from storage → Execute function logic → Update state variables → Consume gas for computation → Write new state to blockchain → Return transaction receipt → END

charts.title: Smart Contract Platforms Comparison
charts.content:
| Feature | Ethereum | Hyperledger Fabric | Cardano | Polkadot |
|----------|----------|-------------------|---------|----------|
| Language | Solidity | Go/Java | Plutus | Rust |
| Consensus | PoW/PoS | PBFT | PoS | PoS |
| Type | Public | Private | Public | Public/Private |
| Turing Complete | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Smart Contract Language | Solidity | Chaincode | Plutus/Marlowe | Ink! |

charts.title: Smart Contract Advantages and Limitations
charts.content:
| Advantages | Limitations |
|-------------|--------------|
| Automation | Code bugs can cause losses |
| Transparency | Cannot access external data without oracles |
| Trustless | Irreversible (no rollback) |
| Cost-effective | Scalability issues |
| Speed | Legal recognition unclear |
| Accuracy | Gas costs can be high |
| Security (immutable) | Upgradability challenges |

branches: Use Cases (DeFi, NFTs, Supply Chain, Voting, Insurance)
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting all aspects of smart contracts for exam preparation
